coredata.dc:title: Solvent extraction-assisted pyrolysis synthesis of N doped citric acid-based carbon dots for ciprofloxacin detection and anti-counterfeiting applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yuexia
Gui, Chaoying
Cui, Xuyan
Zhang, Quanxi
Zheng, Xianyun
Sun, Xuansen
Yang, Zhenhua
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: In this study, citric acid-based N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were rapidly synthesized via open pyrolysis. A facile extraction strategy was proposed to promote the separation of N-CDs by orderly addition of toluene and water. The prepared N-CDs exhibited a high quantum yield (0.53) and excellent dispersibility in solvents of various polarities. The bright blue N-CDs were applied as a fluorescence sensor for ciprofloxacin (CIP) detection due to inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching. The N-CDs sensor exhibited a good two-phase linear relation with CIP with a low detection limit of 0.14 µM. The sensing platform for CIP detection in milk and eye drops was further developed successfully, indicating its potential for CIP detection in real samples. N-CDs-based fluorescent ink was also prepared for anti-counterfeiting. Furthermore, the polyvinyl alcohol/N-CDs film had comparable bright fluorescence with ∼80 % color purity, which further expands the application of N-CDs as photovoltaic materials. The extraction strategy of using different-polarity solvents may open a new way for preparing amphipathic nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Rapid pyrolysis
Detection
Ciprofloxacin
Fluorescent ink

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: N-CDs-based fluorescence sensor for detecting CIP
Analysis of CIP in real samples
Preparation and optical properties of the fluorescent film
Application in fluorescent ink and films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photographs of emulsions of reactive mixtures containing different amounts of oleylamine as well as toluene and water. (b) TEM image of N-CDs in ethanol. (c) Size distribution of N-CDs. (d) Dispersibility of N-CDs in various solvents (DMSO, ethanol, DMF, acetone, toluene, and petroleum ether).
(a) XPS full spectra. High-resolution spectra of C 1s (b), N 1s (c), and O 1s (d). (e) FTIR spectrum of N-CDs.

(a) UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. (b) Excitation-independent fluorescence emission spectra; red ☆ curve shows the emission wavelength related to the excitation wavelength. (c) Photostability of N-CDs. (d) Emission spectra of N-CDs under different pH; colorful ☆ curve shows the dependence of fluorescence intensity on pH. (e) Photographs of blue fluorescence under 365-nm UV irradiation. (f) PL emission of N-CDs in various solvents.

(a) PL spectra of N-CDs for different CIP concentrations. The inset is the plot of a linear relation between ∆F and ciprofloxacin. (b) B–H plot. (c) The heat map of fluorescence responses of N-CDs toward different antibiotics. (d) The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs with mixed antibiotics. Fluorescence sensing of CIP in milk (e) and eye drops (f).

(a) Normalized spectra of UV–vis absorption and excitation/emission. The inset shows the chemical structure of CIP. (b) Normalized spectra of UV–vis absorption of CIP, N-CDs, and N-CDs interaction with CIP. (c) Plot of F0/F versus CIP. (d) Zeta potential of the detection system.

(a) Photographs of hand-written characters and letters on filter paper, rice paper, and stone paper using the fluorescent ink. (b) Anti-counterfeiting using several printed patterns. (c) Stamping patterns using the commercial ink containing a certain amount of fluorescent ink under daylight and 365-nm UV irradiation.
Photographs under daylight (top panel) and 365 nm UV-light (bottom panel) (a). CIE chromaticity diagram (b) and fluorescence emission spectra of different PVA/N-CDs films (c).
Schematic of the synthesis route of N-CDs.
Comparison of literature survey for citric acid-based blue-fluorescent CDs.
Comparison of several CIP detection methods.

head.ce:title: Solvent extraction-assisted pyrolysis synthesis of N doped citric acid-based carbon dots for ciprofloxacin detection and anti-counterfeiting applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental and Resource Sciences, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 03006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental and Resource Sciences, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental and Resource Sciences, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 03006, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Shanxi University, Wucheng Road 92#, Taiyuan, Shanxi Province 030006, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, citric acid-based N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were rapidly synthesized via open pyrolysis. A facile extraction strategy was proposed to promote the separation of N-CDs by orderly addition of toluene and water. The prepared N-CDs exhibited a high quantum yield (0.53) and excellent dispersibility in solvents of various polarities. The bright blue N-CDs were applied as a fluorescence sensor for ciprofloxacin (CIP) detection due to inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching. The N-CDs sensor exhibited a good two-phase linear relation with CIP with a low detection limit of 0.14 µM. The sensing platform for CIP detection in milk and eye drops was further developed successfully, indicating its potential for CIP detection in real samples. N-CDs-based fluorescent ink was also prepared for anti-counterfeiting. Furthermore, the polyvinyl alcohol/N-CDs film had comparable bright fluorescence with ∼80 % color purity, which further expands the application of N-CDs as photovoltaic materials. The extraction strategy of using different-polarity solvents may open a new way for preparing amphipathic nanomaterials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are frequently used in human and veterinary medicine to prevent and treat various infections. Ciprofloxacin (CIP), one of the second-generation fluoroquinolone antibiotics, has excellent antimicrobial activity against infections of the urinary tract, respiratory tract, gastrointestinal tract, skin, and soft tissues [1]. However, the overuse of antibiotics in veterinary medicine generates residues that spread through the food chain, which has raised safety concerns about their toxic effects and bacterial resistance [2]. Badawy et al. found that CIP is unsuitable for pregnant women and children because it causes joint injury [3]. Therefore, a cheap and rapid assay must be urgently developed to detect the concentration of CIP in food. Many efforts have been employed to analyze the CIP concentration, including chromatography [4], electrochemical methods using dual-metal nanocomposites [5], and spectrophotometry [6–9]. However, the associated procedures are usually complicated and require expensive equipment or are time-consuming.

Carbon dots (CDs) have some advantages such as low toxicity, convenient synthesis, and unique optical properties over metal-based nanoparticles [6,10,11]. CDs fluorescence is generally derived from the core, surface state, and embedded fluorophores [12]. Thus, the photoluminescence (PL) properties of CDs mainly depend on the precursors, synthesis methods, doping elements, carbonization temperature, and solvent types [13]; these factors affect the core and surface state of CDs. The core is part of the conjugated network, whereas the surface state is dictated by multiple functional groups on the surface of CDs [14]. Thus, surface state modification is a practical approach for tuning the optical properties of CDs. Since CDs possess tunable and functional PL, they can serve as optical probes for detecting environmental pollutants, including CIP, and as optoelectronic materials in environmental and chemical fields.

Information security is a crucial aspect of modern life [5,15]. To avoid information disclosure, labels that are invisible to the naked eye are inevitable in many fields. Thus, anti-counterfeiting materials have garnered considerable attention in several domains such as logical gates, sensors, and security warning systems [15]. Moreover, advanced encryption and anti-counterfeiting methods are urgently required to protect valuable products. As CDs are excellent optical materials, they could be explored for use as photovoltaic and anti-counterfeiting materials.

The design of fluorescent CDs for the detection of environmental pollutants is of considerable importance, for which bottom-up synthesis approaches such as hydrothermal carbonization, microwave-assisted synthesis, and pyrolysis are widely used. CDs with excellent optical properties can be developed using citric acid as the carbon source. Gu et al. [16] synthesized the CDs using citric acid and urea as carbon and nitrogen sources via a hydrothermal reaction. However, open pyrolysis is a considerably safer and more efficient approach for synthesizing CDs to achieve the thermal decomposition of a wide range of raw materials [17]. In our previous study, oil-soluble CDs (O-CDs) were synthesized via pyrolysis using citric acid, tris, and oleylamine as precursors [18].

Herein, bright blue N-doped CDs (N-CDs) was prepared via a pyrolysis process and a novel extraction strategy is introduced to enhance the separation of CDs using toluene and water, facilitating the formation of amphipathic carbon dots. The proposed method is faster than other reported methods. N-CDs with a higher quantum yield (QY) of 0.53 were obtained that were soluble in various organic solvents. Based on the fluorescence quenching effect of CIP on N-CDs, a novel fluorescence sensing method was successfully developed. N-CDs can also be used as fluorescent inks and fluorescent flexible films. These findings show that N-CDs have potential applications in CIP detection and anti-counterfeiting.

Oleylamine was purchased from Jiangshun Chemical Technology Company (Guangzhou, China). Other chemical materials were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Company (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Double-distilled deionized water was used throughout this study.

N-CDs were synthesized via open pyrolysis from citric acid, tris, and oleylamine as the precursors. In a typical experiment, 2.00 g of citric acid and 0.25 g of tris were placed in a 100-mL beaker and heated at 190℃ for 11 min. Then, a certain amount of oleylamine (0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, or 0.9 mL) was added to the aforementioned solution to optimize the N-CDs. The resulting mixture was heated and stirred continuously for 1 min. Subsequently, toluene and water were added orderly and then stirred vigorously. After overnight standing of the mixture, the solutions on the upper and bottom were discarded; the emulsion of the middle layer was centrifugated and dialyzed and then freeze-dried to obtain N-CDs, which were stored at 4℃ for further characterization and applications. The QY of N-CDs was evaluated using quinine sulfate as a reference standard and calculated according to the method in the previous report[18].

The size and morphology of N-CDs were obtained using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with a JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan). AXIS ULTRA DLD X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Kratos, Japan) and Bruker Tensorll Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) (BRUKER, German) were used to analyze the elemental components and structure of N-CDs. Fluorescence lifetimes were determined using FLS920 (Edinburgh, UK). Steady-state fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using the Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA) and Lambda 365 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA), respectively.

The fluorescence spectra of N-CDs were measured under different time and pH values to examine the optical stability of N-CDs. A series of the B-R buffer solutions of different pH were prepared. The pH values were adjusted using NaOH solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-CDs-based fluorescence sensor for detecting CIP
ce:section.ce:para: The CIP detection was carried out in the N-CDs solution. Different volumes of 0.1 M CIP were added to 2 mL of 1 mg/mL N-CDs solution. After thoroughly mixing, the PL intensity was collected. The optimal condition focused on different reaction time for detecting CIP. To determine the selectivity of N-CDs sensor, CIP was replaced by various antibiotics during the detection process, where the concentration of each antibiotic was 10 μM. To verify the anti-interference capability of the sensor, CIP was added to the N-CDs containing different antibiotics. Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs at 406 nm were recorded. The detection limit of the method was calculated using the formula (3σ/s), where σ is the standard deviation of blank measurements (n = 10) and s is the slope of the calibration curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of CIP in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The milk sample was obtained from a local shop in Shanxi University. The milk was pretreated based on conditions outlined in a previous study with minor modifications [19]. Briefly, four aliquots of 1.0 mL of acetonitrile were gradually mixed with 2.0 mL of milk to precipitate the proteins. After mixing for 15 min, 1.00 g of NaCl was added and the mixture was vortexed for another 15 min. The resulting milk sample was centrifuged at 3000 rpm and 4℃ for 5 min to remove any deposits. In addition, the eye drops were centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min and then filtrated through a 0.22 µM microporous membrane for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and optical properties of the fluorescent film
ce:section.ce:para: Further, 3.00 g of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was mixed with 3 mL of ultrapure water, and the obtained mixture was stirred vigorously at 85℃ until complete dissolution. Then, the PVA solution was added to 3 mL of N-CD solution. Finally, the obtained PVA/N-CDs composite was transferred to a clean Petri dish and dried at 45℃ for 12 h for water evaporation. PVA/N-CDs composites of different concentrations (10, 20, and 50 mg/mL) were fabricated.

The N-CDs solution (5 mg/mL) was filled into an empty pen tube without any modifications as an anti-counterfeiting fluorescence ink. A filter paper with a specific pattern was used for information encryption modeling. In another experiment, 10 % PVA was prepared in hot water. Then, 10 mL of PVA solution was mixed with the N-CDs solution. After ultrasonication for 15 min, the obtained fluorescent ink was obtained for subsequent experiments, and pictures written or drawn on various matrixes were captured using a digital camera.
All results are presented as the means ± standard deviation (SD) for quantitative data. All statistical data processing in this work was carried out using SPSS 19.0 software.

Based on O-CD synthesis [18], N-CDs were successfully fabricated via open pyrolysis from citric acid, tris, and oleylamine using a toluene/water-assisted separation method ( Scheme 1). The carboxyl and amino groups from these raw materials may have facilitated carbonization [20]. The great difference in the polarity of toluene and water is used to exact N-CDs from a crude reaction mixture. After pyrolysis and carbonization were ceased, toluene and water were orderly added into the reaction solution for the formation of emulsion ( Fig. 1a). The turbid mixture is thoroughly stirred and divided into three layers. The blue-fluorescent solutions in the upper and bottom layers were abandoned. N-CDs were obtained from the emulsion in the middle layer. The new extraction method allows the formation of amphiphilic N-CDs. The post-functionalization using oleyamine is simpler and faster than that via dialysis in the previous study[21]. Unfortunately, the N-CDs had a small solubility in aqueous solution due to the modification of oleylamine on the surface of N-CDs. Since the amount of oleylamine affects the harvest of N-CDs, a series of experiments were conducted to optimize the synthesis of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1a, the optimal amounts of N-CDs were obtained using 0.7 mL of oleylamine compared with other oleylamine dosages. Citric acid and other reactants were frequently introduced to synthesize citric acid-based N-CDs in previous studies ( Table 1). Obviously, the citric acid-based N-CDs synthesized using close pyrolysis and hydrothermal methods require high pressure and longer reaction times[22–27], which limit their mass production. Contrarily, the synthesis methods used herein considerably reduced the reaction time and did not require high pressures, making them superior to previously reported methods.

The TEM image and particle size distribution of N-CDs in ethanol are shown in Fig. 1b. N-CDs are spherical with an average size of 3.49 nm (Fig. 1c), larger than the O-CDs [18]. The dispersibility of N-CDs was examined by dissolving them in various solvents (Fig. 1d). The N-CDs uniformly dispersed in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethanol, dimethyl formamide (DMF), acetone, toluene, and petroleum ether, confirming the amphiphilic feature of N-CDs. The N-CDs dispersibility in various solvents is greatly improved due to post-treatment of pyrolysis reaction while O-CDs without post-treatment have a poor hydrophilic capability. The difference in dispersibility between the N-CDs and O-CDs may be attributed to the difference in their surface states.

The N-CDs were further characterized by XPS and FTIR spectra to determine their chemical compositions and functional groups. The XPS full spectrum shown in Fig. 2a has three peaks at 283, 398, and 528 eV, attributed to C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s, respectively. The corresponding element contents of N-CDs are 50.35 % for C, 14.86 % for N, and 34.80 % for O. The N-CDs contain higher N content than O-CDs. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra show distinct peaks corresponding to CC/C-C, C-N/C-O, and CO functional groups at 284.7, 285.4, and 288.4 eV [20], respectively. The N 1s spectrum shows two peaks at 399.3 and 400.2 eV assigned to the pyridinic N and amino N groups[29], respectively. This indicates the formation of pyridinic derivatives [30]. The O 1s spectrum is decomposed into peaks at 530.5, 531.4, and 535.8 eV, corresponding to CO, C-OH, and, C-O-C, respectively[29]. The FTIR spectrum in Fig. 2e provides additional structural information on N-CDs. The broad peak at 3406 cm−1 is attributed to the O-H/N-H stretching vibration. The two peaks at 2923 and 1445 cm−1 correspond to the C-H and C-N stretching vibrations, respectively [29]. The peak at 1771 cm−1 is attributed to the CO stretching vibration and those at 1578 and 1071 cm−1 correspond to the CN and C–O bending vibrations [31], which is in agreement with XPS results. Furthermore, the characteristic peak at 841 cm−1 is assigned to the C-H out-of-plane bending vibration of aromatic benzene. The N-CDs have a higher C-N absorption peak than the O-CDs, which agrees with the XPS results. The FTIR and XPS results confirm the successful doping of nitrogen into the frame of N-CDs and the existence of abundant nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups, including -OH.

The optical properties of N-CDs are important for their application; therefore, UV–vis and steady-state fluorescence spectra were recorded at optimal excitation wavelengths ( Fig. 3a). The absorption spectrum shows a characteristic peak at 334 nm assigned to the n–π* transition at the edge of the carbon lattice, which occurs due to the electronic transition of functional groups on the CDs surface [32]. Instead, the characteristic absorption bands of the starting materials are not observed, suggesting the role of N doping. The excitation spectrum comprises two convolution bands at 230 and 329 nm. The emission band is centered at 406 and has an asymmetric peak with a trailing tail at the low-energy edge due to environmentally sensitive d–f transition [33]. Previous studies show that citric acid and amino-contaning species easily form pyridine derivatives, which can attach to carbon core[30]. The absorption and optical properties are similar to that of these species, suggesting the possibility of pyridine molecules. Generally, the practical applications of conventional CDs are limited by their broad luminescence band with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of > 80 nm [34]. Fortunately, the as-prepared N-CDs, however, have a narrower FWHM (65 nm) (Fig. S1). The band gap energy (Eg) was further calculated using the classical formula E = 1240/λ edge [35]. The Eg of N-CDs (406 nm) was calculated as 6.01 eV, similar to other blue CDs [35]. The emission peak positions excited at different excitation wavelengths were unchanged (Fig. 3b), which is a classical optical property of CDs [17]. The previous evidence shows that the PL behavior of CDs is attributed to their surface state rather than particle size [36]. The non-uniform sizes of the N-CDs suggest that the excitation-independent feature is caused by uniform surface states[36]. Furthermore, the QY of N-CDs with 0.53 is comparable to the previous reports [13,22,25–27] and higher than that of O-CDs due to their higher nitrogen content [27].

The photostability of N-CDs was also examined under UV irradiation. No obvious photobleaching phenomenon was observed when the N-CDs were subjected to 90 min of continuous UV exposure (Fig. 3c), proving their excellent photostability. The chemical nature of N-CDs was further verified by investigating the effect of pH on the emission spectra of the as-synthesized N-CDs in ethanol (Fig. 3d). The blue-shift fluorescence peaks of N-CDs are observed from 430 to 406 nm, and the fluorescence is gradually enhanced at pH 2–7. The change in the blue-shift emission of as-prepared N-CDs is consistent with carboxylic acids (pKa ≈ 4–5) on the surface of N-CDs [22]. As the pH increases from 7 to 11, the fluorescence intensities at 406 nm show negligible change. The emission intensity sharply decreases at the highest pH and blue-shifts to 403 nm because some oxygen-rich groups, such as -COOH and -OH, deprotonate [35]. The as-prepared N-CDs in various organic solvents emit blue fluorescence under 365-nm UV irradiation (Fig. 3e–f). In addition, the PL of N-CDs is strongly affected by the solvent environment. The fluorescence maxima decreases as the polarity of solvents decreases from ethanol to petroleum ether, possibly because of the presence of -OH and -NH2 functional groups on the N-CDs surface [37].

Previous studies have shown that CIP can be transferred to human from food chain, consequently exerting some adverse health effects [2,3]. A simple, reliable, and highly sensitive method must be developed for the real-time detection of CIP in food. N-CDs exhibit excellent optical properties at pH 7; therefore, a detection platform was developed at this pH. The optical signal of the detection platform remained unchanged in the range of 0–10 min (Fig. S2), suggesting the sensor is sensitive and rapid for CIP. As shown in Fig. 4a, the FL intensity of N-CDs decreases as the CIP concentration increases from 5.0 to 50.1 μM and exhibit a two-phase linear relationship to CIP concentrations of 5.0–16.7 and 16.7–50.1 μM. Further, limits of detection (LOD) were determined to be 0.14 and 0.31 μM. Obvisouly, N-CDs was more sentitive to the CIP at low concentration than high concentration, which may due to the fact that the binding site on the surface of N-CDs tend to be saturated at high concentration of CIP. The association constant was calculated as 2.15 × 10−4 M−1 from the intercept and slope of the linear fitted equation (Fig. 4b) according to the modified Benesi–Hildebrand plot [38]. Nevertheless, the complex ingredients of real samples pose a great challenge to the fluorescence sensor [39]. Other antibiotics were selected to verify the selectivity of the N-CDs-based sensor. As shown in Fig. 4c and d, the difference in the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs with CIP and/or other antibiotics can be distinguished significantly, indicating the superior selectivity and sensitivity of N-CDs for CIP detection. To evaluate the feasibility and reliability of the proposed method, spiked recovery experiments were conducted to determine the CIP content in milk and eye drops. As depicted in Fig. 4e and f, the recovery rates range from 98.2 % to 102.3 % with relative standard deviation (RSD) values between 0.47 % and 1.59 %. These findings confirm the possibility of the proposed method in real samples.

The acceptable daily intake is 100 µg/kg in milk reported by the European Food Safety Authority [40]. The developed sensor can detect CIP levels below the harmful concentration. Compared with previously reported fluorescence assays ( Table 2), the fluorescent sensor displays a comparable or even superior LOD/linear range [7–9]. Some studies also reported lower LOD values [1,41,42]. However, such sensors have complex and time-consuming detection processes and require expensive equipment as well as toxic materials and dyes for material synthesis, which limits their real-time application.

To reveal the interaction mechanism between CIP and N-CDs, the difference in the lifetime emission, absorption spectra, lifetime, and zeta potential of N-CDs with different CIP concentrations were investigated. The sufficient overlapping of excitation/emission bands of the fluorophore with the absorption band of the absorber decreases the fluorescence intensity of the fluorophore due to the inner filter effect (IFE) [7]. In this work, CIP, a type of fluoroquinolone antibiotic with β-diketone moiety, can act as an absorber for the sensing platform of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 5a, the CIP absorption band partly intersects with the excitation/emission bands, revealing that the CIP can quench the fluorescence of N-CDs owing to IFE. In static quenching, the fluorophore and quencher have a chemical interaction, which inhibits the formation of an excited state. Fluorescence lifetime is an important parameter that is characteristic of fluorescence molecules. A molecule containing multiple fluorophores yields multiexponential fluorescence lifetime values [44]. The time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of N-CDs before and after the addition of CIP were fitted with a double exponential function, as shown in Fig. S3. The two fluorescence lifetimes of pure N-CDs reveal that their luminescence is attributed to the carbon core and surface states. On interacting with 8.35, 16.70, and 33.40 μM of CIP, the fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs remained almost unchanged, clearly indicating the role of static quenching [7]. Additionally, after the interaction of CIP with the N-CDs, the absorption spectra became closer to the CIP absorption (Fig. 5b), indicating that CIP can absorb excitation photons and cause quenching. KSV, a quenching constant, was determined as 2.46 × 104 M−1 from the modified Stern–Volmer curve (Fig. 5c). Kq was calculated to be 1.01 × 1013 M−1 s−1 using formula 1 (Supporting Information). Furthermore, the zeta potential of the sensor for different CIP concentrations was examined. As shown in Fig. 5d, the zeta potential of N-CDs is −16.9 mV, confirming the presence of negative charges on their surface; this finding agrees with the FTIR results. The zeta potential of the detection platform is more negative than N-CDs, indicating that the electrostatic interaction between the N-CDs-based sensor and CIP is excluded. Thus, we infer that fluorescence resonance energy transfer cannot occur in the N-CDs-based sensing platform. This further confirms that IFE and static quenching were involved in the interaction between N-CDs and CIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in fluorescent ink and films
ce:section.ce:para: Anti-counterfeiting fluorescent inks exhibit distinct optical properties under different conditions. As N-CDs have excellent optical properties, they can serve as fluorescence ink. The N-CDs solution is used as a fluorescence ink for writing Chinese characters on various types of paper including filter paper, rice paper, and stone paper ( Fig. 6a). Although the Chinese characters and “SXU” pattern drawn are invisible to the naked eye, all the patterns are visible under 365-nm UV irradiation. Moreover, pre-designed patterns were printed onto unbleached paper, and the outlines of some patterns were drawn using the N-CDs fluorescent ink. Fig. 6b shows the blue digital and flower patterns drawn on a nonfluorescence paper under 365-nm UV irradiation. These results verify the practicability of N-CDs as an anticounterfeiting ink. In another experiment, a certain amount of N-CDs (0, 30, 50, and 70 %) was mixed with commercial ink. The rabbit stamps using a mixture of commercial ink and N-CDs-based ink emitted blue fluorescence under 365-nm UV irradiation and appeared dark under daylight. Since the N-CDs display strong fluorescence in various matrices and commercial ink, the N-CDs-based fluorescence ink is feasible and practical compared with the previous study[36,45]. These findings reveal that the N-CDs-based fluorescence ink can be used for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.

In addition, CDs are embedded in a solid matrix for use in down-conversion LEDs, photovoltaics, and anticounterfeiting materials [29]. PVA mixed with CDs can effectively disperse CDs and inhibit nonradiative decaying and luminescence quenching caused by CDs aggregation. Herein, the possibility of using N-CDs as a phototype of CDs-based optical materials such as LEDs and anti-counterfeiting labels is examined. PVA/N-CDs fluorescent films exhibit excellent transparency and pliability (Fig. S4). Under UV irradiation, various concentrations (0, 10, 20, and 50 mg/mL) of PVA/N-CDs composites emit blue fluorescence; however, they appear colorless under daylight ( Fig. 7a). The fluorescence intensity of the PVA/N-CDs film is enhanced as the N-CDs content increases. The chromaticity coordinates of N-CDs were calculated from the fluorescence spectra. The Commission Internationale de l´Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of the fluorescent film under D65 light and 365-nm UV irradiation are shown in Table S1. The chromaticity coordinates of PVA/N-CDs films under daylight are relatively stable from (0.322, 0.329) to (0.343, 0.356). The CIE coordinates under 365-nm UV light are located in the blue region in the CIE diagram from (0.174, 0.129) to (0.162, 0.104) (Fig. 7b). Furthermore, the color purity of N-CDs can be calculated using the following formula (1) Tang et al. [33].

−
y
i
)
2
× 100 %
Where (x, y), (xd, yd), and (xi, yi) are the CIE color coordinates of the synthesized N-CDs, the corresponding dominant wavelength light source, and the standard white light source, respectively. According to formula (1), the color purities of PVA/N-CDs films containing different N-CDs contents are 70.88 %, 72.23 %, and 78.33 %, respectively. The maximum PL emission peaks of PVA/N-CDs films excited at 370 nm are centered at 398, 407, and 413 nm, corresponding to blue fluorescence (Fig. 7c). Moreover, the maximum emission wavelength changes are indicative of concentration-induced stacking. This further expands the application of N-CDs as photoelectric material.

Herein, citric acid-based N-CDs with narrower FWHM and high QY were fabricated via rapid condensation and carbonization between citric acid, tris, and oleylamine. The N-CDs exhibited excellent photostability and monodispersed in various organic solvents due to the presence of hydrophilic groups and a long alkyl chain. The N-CDs can be used for CIP detection in milk and eye drops. Moreover, they can be directly used as fluorescence inks for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption. The PVA/N-CDs composites exhibit bright blue fluorescence, comparable to the luminescence of organic solvents. Therefore, N-CDs with a high color purity are promising nanomaterials in diverse fields.

Cui Xuyan: Investigation, Data curation. Gui Chaoying: Investigation, Data curation. Zheng Xianyun: Funding acquisition. Zhang Quanxi: Funding acquisition. Zhang Yuexia: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Conceptualization. Yang Zhenhua: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Sun Xuansen: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Dong Chuan: Writing – review & editing, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 42477031), the Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (Nos. 2023-030) and the Applied Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province (20210302123472) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.mtcomm.2025.112392.
